The model treated a negative maximum-allotment-minus-expected-contribution calculation as a zero benefit. For this categorically eligible elderly/disabled household, the negative standard calculation triggers the monthly minimum allotment, totaling $287.68 annually.

The model applied an inapplicable higher Pennsylvania CHIP income range and concluded that $61,277 was below the relevant ceiling. Under the applicable CHIP threshold, Child 1 fails the income test and is also not eligible through Medicaid, yielding no CHIP eligibility.

The model applied an erroneously high Pennsylvania CHIP income ceiling and treated age plus income as sufficient. The model incorrectly treated the EITC as fully phased out at this income and therefore omitted the $624.13 EITC. Adding that phaseout-range EITC to its correctly identified $2,650.80 refundable CTC yields $3,274.93.

The model correctly calculated $3,720 of Social Security tax and $870 of Medicare tax but computed Pennsylvania's employee unemployment compensation contribution as $7 instead of $42. The model omitted Pennsylvania’s refundable state EITC and incorrectly concluded that no refundable credit applied. Applying Pennsylvania’s 10% rate to the household’s $624.13 federal EITC yields $62.41.

The model incorrectly counted the $50,000 of financial assistance as Medicaid MAGI. Only the $312 of taxable interest enters this household's MAGI, placing the head below Oklahoma's adult expansion threshold and making the head Medicaid eligible.

The model treated the ordinary one-person gross-income limit as dispositive and omitted categorical eligibility through TANF non-cash benefits. It also converted an eligible household's benefit to zero when the expected contribution exceeded the maximum allotment instead of applying the one-person minimum allotment.

The model compared gross annual retirement income directly with California's aged-or-disabled Medicaid income threshold. It omitted the required deduction for health insurance premiums, including Medicare Part B, which reduces countable income below the limit.

The model applied Minnesota's CHIP income ceiling without first testing Medicaid eligibility. Child 1 qualifies for Medicaid under the OLDER_CHILD category, and that Medicaid eligibility makes the child ineligible for CHIP.

The model applied an incorrect boundary between Minnesota Medicaid and CHIP for the older-child age category. At 156% of the federal poverty level, the six-year-old is within the applicable Medicaid income limit; the separate CHIP range begins only above that limit.

The model applied only the federal income threshold and omitted Minnesota's universal free school meals pathway. Because the six-year-old is enrolled in K-12 under the benchmark inputs, the state program produces positive annual free-meal support.

The model explicitly excluded state payroll tax and stopped after federal FICA of $2,218.50. It omitted Minnesota’s $127.60 employee paid leave contribution on $29,000 of Minnesota paid-leave taxable wages.

The model applied only a federal reduced-price income threshold and inferred positive reduced-price support from it. It omitted Minnesota's universal free school meals pathway, which classifies the household as FREE and therefore produces no reduced-price support.

The model applied Minnesota's adult Medicaid income limit solely because the person is 18. PolicyEngine classifies an 18-year-old under the OLDER_CHILD pathway, whose income threshold includes MAGI at 156% of the federal poverty level, yielding eligibility.

The model treated Minnesota’s Working Family Credit as 25% of an estimated federal EITC and added an unphased $1,750 child credit. Minnesota’s 2026 combined CWFC calculation instead applies its own earnings and AGI rules and phaseout, yielding $2,187.60 for one eligible child.

The model's taxable income of about $35,951 includes the $243 state and local tax refund as taxable income. The tax-benefit rule excludes that refund because no prior-year itemized-deduction benefit is present, reducing taxable income to $35,708.23 and tax to $3,788.99.

The model applied New Jersey's pension exclusion based solely on the income threshold and omitted the requirement that the taxpayer be age 62 or older or disabled. The model used $8,231 as the EITC maximum, applying the wrong maximum instead of the 2026 two-child maximum of $7,316. The model applied the earned-income and standard deductions only approximately, producing an expected contribution that was $29.94 too low over the year. Using the engine’s exact net-income deduction result and 30% contribution produces annual SNAP of $7,286.94, not $7,316.88.

The model correctly applied Louisiana’s 5% rate but overstated the federal EITC as $8,231 instead of $7,316. It failed to compute the federal EITC limitation for this joint-filing household’s earned income, so its inflated federal-credit base produced $411.55 rather than $365.80.

The model treated the surviving-spouse indicator as sufficient for qualifying-surviving-spouse joint filing status despite the absence of a qualifying dependent, then included $25,950 of estate income in AGI. The model treated the disability flag itself as sufficient for Medicare eligibility. It failed to require an under-65 Medicare pathway—such as qualifying disability-benefit entitlement with the required waiting period, ESRD, or ALS—so the age-61 head remains ineligible.

The model constructed Virginia income from the listed gross income streams instead of beginning with federal AGI of $32,699.07, thereby treating the $25,950 estate income as part of the Virginia tax base. The model overstated AGI at about $58,949 instead of $43,637.65 by including income beyond the traced taxable components and overstating taxable Social Security. The model stopped at an overstated pre-credit tax estimate and explicitly failed to identify the $300 Wisconsin property tax credit generated by the taxpayer's $5,010.88 of real estate taxes. It also failed to reproduce the prescribed Wisconsin AGI, standard deduction, and exemption calculation that yields $584.74 before that credit and $284.74 afterward.

The model correctly reduced SNAP net income to zero but treated $298 as the maximum allotment for every month of 2026. It failed to apply the later monthly maximum of $304.68, so its flat $298 × 12 annualization understated the benefit by $20.04.

The model omitted Colorado's sales tax refund and therefore incorrectly concluded that no refundable state credit applied. The filer's age of 66 and qualifying employment income establish eligibility, and MAGI of $4,625.40 yields a $19 refund.

The model treated the one-person gross-income threshold as an automatic bar and failed to apply categorical eligibility through TANF non-cash assistance. It therefore never reached the net-income deductions and minimum-allotment floor that produce $287.68 annually.

The model used a $5,800 personal exemption instead of $5,950 and a 4.05% rate instead of Michigan's applicable 4.25% rate. The correct computation applies 4.25% to $36,275.59 minus $5,950, yielding $1,288.84.

The model substituted an overtime deduction and the Oklahoma standard deduction for the household's larger $22,429.78 itemized deduction, failing to derive $75,303 of Oklahoma taxable income. The model incorrectly deducted the $580 charitable contribution and $928 of vehicle-loan interest in addition to the standard deduction. The model correctly computed $5,021 after the joint-filer exemption but immediately applied the 3% rate. It omitted the New Hampshire filing-threshold test, under which $5,021 produces no liability.

The model incorrectly added the $180 non-Schedule-D capital gains input to the $40,000 of IRS gross income, producing taxable income of $24,080 instead of $23,900. Applying the brackets to that extra $180 overstated tax by $21.60, yielding $2,641.60 rather than $2,620.

The model's $472,622 taxable-income estimate omitted the $43.28 deductible traditional IRA contribution, leaving taxable income about $43 too high. The model applied Idaho’s 5.3% rate to all $50,869 of taxable income and omitted the initial zero-tax bracket. That flat-rate shortcut produced $2,696.06 instead of applying the bracket structure.

The model incorrectly imposed an income cutoff or phase-out on Idaho's grocery credit at $66,969 of wages. No reduction applies to this household, so the full-year $155 base credit remains refundable.

The model applied the EITC phase-in and refundable CTC phase-in directly to the full $17,100 of self-employment income. It failed to subtract the deductible half of self-employment tax from earned income, causing both its $6,840 EITC and $2,190 refundable CTC to be overstated.

The model applied the ordinary SNAP resource limit to the $14,000 bank balance and ignored the household's broad-based categorical eligibility through TANF non-cash assistance. That categorical pathway eliminates the asset disqualification, leaving the household eligible for $6,125.69 annually.
The model substituted a zero placeholder instead of calculating the liability. The traced gross income, deductions, and 2026 brackets produce $25,238.58, not zero.

The model treated the absence of an explicitly supplied payroll-tax amount as grounds for a zero placeholder instead of calculating tax from the listed $83,635 of annual wages. The model treated the listed financial assistance as countable SNAP income, driving net income and the calculated allotment to zero. The model incorrectly denied the New Jersey childless EITC. At age 20 with qualifying income and $4,033.39 of earned income, the filer enters New Jersey’s childless-credit phase-in and receives $265.60 after the applicable reduction.
The model used the stale $649 maximum for the childless-worker EITC instead of the 2026 maximum of $664. Its eligibility conclusion was correct, but it applied the wrong tax-year parameter.

The model over-deducted income to reach a $340 monthly allotment, which implies an expected contribution of only $206 and net income near $687 rather than $1,090.08. The allowable earned-income, standard, and medical deductions do not reduce this household’s countable income that far.

The model treated both adults as SSI-eligible solely because both were described as disabled, then applied the eligible-couple federal benefit rate. The spouse does not meet SSI eligibility criteria; only the head receives SSI, with spousal deeming producing $391.42 in monthly countable income and a $7,231 annual benefit.
The model incorrectly treated having a qualifying child as necessary for Louisiana's refundable EITC. Louisiana applies its 5% rate to the household's $664 childless federal EITC, yielding $33.20.

The model explicitly included the $28,800 survivor-benefits amount in taxable income even though it is not taxable federal income. The model applied unspecified household income and resource rules instead of the adult-expansion MAGI pathway. Dependent1's own listed MAGI is zero, and Wisconsin's expansion adult test covers a 27-year-old at 0% of FPL without an asset test.

The model understated the tax generated by $67,794.08 of taxable income. After the deductions it named, the 2026 joint rate schedule produces $7,639.29 before credits; subtracting the applicable $3,200 of nonrefundable dependent credits yields $4,439.29, not $2,219.

The model's $2,800 estimate does not apply the Wisconsin joint-filer schedule to the correctly derived $102,144.25 of taxable income. The model incorrectly treated having no dependents as disqualifying the taxpayer from Virginia’s refundable EITC. The household qualifies for a $39.78 federal childless-worker EITC, and Virginia refunds 20% of that amount, yielding $7.96.

The model incorrectly treated $67,750 of educational assistance as federally taxable income after applying a $5,250 exclusion. PolicyEngine excludes the educational assistance input from AGI here, leaving only employment income before the standard deduction.

The model correctly approximated taxable income and bracket tax but incorrectly declared that no nonrefundable credits were available. The model used a $2,850 Illinois personal exemption instead of the 2026 amount of $2,925. That made its tax base $33,420 rather than $33,344.67 and produced $1,654.29 instead of $1,650.56 at the 4.95% rate.

The model subtracted only two ordinary personal exemptions and omitted Michigan's Tier 1 senior standard deduction for a joint return with both spouses born before 1946. The model incorrectly treated the household as lacking disability status despite the head’s Social Security disability income and concluded that the gross-income test barred eligibility. The model treated the disability flag itself as sufficient for Medicare eligibility. At age 62, the head lacks the required under-65 Medicare pathway because SSDI entitlement and its waiting period, end-stage renal disease, and other qualifying statuses are all unlisted and therefore false.

The model incorrectly used married-filing-jointly status and a $32,200 standard deduction even though no spouse is listed and the head has two qualifying children, requiring head-of-household treatment. It also omitted the $800 qualified business income deduction; applying the $24,150 head-of-household standard deduction, the QBI deduction, head-of-household brackets, and $4,600 of nonrefundable credits yields $19,611.80.

The model reduced taxable income to $132,020 instead of $138,270, omitting or miscomputing the traced AGI-to-taxable-income deductions. It then subtracted an invented $440 Idaho child credit instead of applying the head-of-household schedule’s $9,840 zero-rate bracket.
The model omitted Idaho's refundable grocery credit entirely. The head and both children each qualify for the full-year $155 credit, producing $465.
The model used a $12,500 Louisiana standard deduction instead of $12,835. That inflated taxable income from $10,171.18 to about $10,506 and produced $315.18 rather than $305.14 at the 3% rate.

The model did not apply the correct $36,233.77 SALT deduction under the 2026 rules. Its higher tax result reflects taxable income above the correct $134,061.22 produced by the full $64,443.77 itemized deduction.

The model awarded the full $546 monthly maximum despite the household having $1,157 of SNAP net income. It omitted the approximately $347 expected monthly contribution that must be subtracted from the maximum allotment and also treated $546 as constant across all twelve months.

The model used a $1,487 monthly SSI couple maximum instead of $1,491. Its income reduction was otherwise $610 per month, so the $4 monthly parameter error produced $877 monthly and understated annual SSI by $48.

The model incorrectly required tax liability, earned income, or qualifying children for an Arizona refundable credit. It omitted the increased excise tax credit, which provides this qualifying married-filing-jointly two-adult Arizona tax unit $50.

The model treated all $4,540—$3,600 of assistance, $924 of capital gains, and $16 of dividends—as countable annual SNAP income, improperly including the capital gain as recurring income. The model incorrectly subtracted the separately listed $7,389 employer-sponsored insurance premium from the stated $175,002 gross wages, even though the wage input enters AGI in full under the benchmark facts. PolicyEngine instead computes AGI of $174,088.12 before deducting $31,028.65 of itemized deductions and $1.24 of QBI deduction.

The model incorrectly subtracted a capped renter deduction and then approximated the liability as 5% of the reduced aggregate income. The correct Part B base is $164,658 after only the traced $4,400 personal exemption, and the separate Part A tax on $110.12 of dividends must also be included.

The model reduced AGI to about $89,951 by treating the full net capital loss as currently deductible instead of applying the annual capital-loss deduction limit and carrying forward the excess. That understated taxable income relative to the correct $92,735.15 and therefore understated the tax.

The model included the $31.20 New York disability contribution but omitted the $411.91 mandatory New York paid family leave contribution. Adding that omitted state payroll tax to its $7,696.12 total yields $8,108.03.

The model materially understated the New York tax base instead of carrying the $117,585.15 AGI through the $11,200 standard deduction and $1,000 exemption to $105,385.15 of taxable income. The model awarded the full $1,000 New York Child Tax Credit and omitted both the $709.50 AGI-based phase-out and the separate $360 Child and Dependent Care Credit. Those steps yield $290.50 plus $360, not $1,000.

The model calculated a benefit solely from monthly countable income and omitted Texas’s SNAP resource test. The household’s $6,620 in bank-account assets exceeds the $5,000 resource limit, making it ineligible and reducing annual SNAP to $0 before the allotment formula is applied.

The model treated receipt of Social Security disability income as immediate Medicare eligibility. At age 62, the head does not qualify through age, and the facts do not establish completion of the 24-month disability-benefit waiting period or another under-65 Medicare pathway.
The model stopped after applying Pennsylvania’s 3.07% rate to $1,675 of taxable interest. It failed to apply Pennsylvania tax forgiveness, which reduces the resulting $51.42 liability to $0.

The model treated only $6,412—half of the $12,824 Social Security benefit—as taxable. The provisional-income calculation places $10,900.40, or 85%, of those benefits in gross income, so the model understated taxable income and federal tax.

The model treated the $1,700 refundable CTC cap as an automatic payment for any qualifying child. It failed to apply the ordering between the nonrefundable CTC and refundable additional CTC: this household uses its available CTC against positive pre-credit tax liability, leaving refundable CTC of $0.

The model correctly recognized Georgia's Social Security subtraction but did not apply the 2026 $15,000 head-of-household standard deduction and $5,000 dependent exemption to reach taxable income of $41,662.50. Its $1,357.80 result reflects an incorrect deduction-and-rate calculation rather than Georgia's applicable tax schedule.

The model applied the disabled-household net-income and excess-shelter-deduction rules but omitted the SNAP resource test. The $31,776 bank balance makes the household ineligible, so mortgage interest and property taxes cannot generate the $298 monthly maximum allotment.

The model omitted the $9,568.44 qualified business income deduction from its stated deduction sequence and consequently overstated taxable income at about $62,996 instead of $60,964.27. Applying the brackets to that overstated base produced $7,064 rather than $6,819.71.

The model incorrectly subtracted the $7,789 employer-sponsored insurance premium from the spouse’s $65,881 of wages when determining the FICA base. Using the resulting $58,092 base produced $4,444, whereas employee Social Security and Medicare taxes apply to the full $65,881, producing $5,039.91.

The model reduced North Carolina taxable income to about $69,696 instead of $80,700.13, taking roughly $11,004 of unsupported additional deductions beyond the $25,500 joint standard deduction. North Carolina tax is $80,700.13 × 3.99% = $3,219.94.

The model applied the childless EITC's 7.65% phase-in rate without first enforcing its minimum-age requirement. At age 22, the taxpayer is ineligible for the childless EITC, so multiplying $7,000 by 7.65% incorrectly produced $535.50 instead of $0.

The model applied an inadequate Kansas standard deduction and personal-exemption total, producing $1,120 of taxable income when the applicable 2026 standard deduction reduces taxable income to zero. Its $58.24 answer then incorrectly taxes that nonexistent base at 5.2%.

The model calculated a childless federal EITC of $535.50 without applying the minimum age of 25. Because the 22-year-old head has no qualifying child, the federal EITC is zero, leaving no amount to which Kansas’s 17% state EITC rate can apply.

The model treated enrollment and documentation eligibility as sufficient for an American Opportunity Tax Credit, despite the prompt setting unlisted qualified education expenses to zero. The model awarded the maximum $1,000 refundable American Opportunity Credit solely from the student and documentation facts. It omitted the required qualified-education-expense base, which is zero under the prompt’s unlisted-input rule, making both the total AOC and its 40% refundable portion zero.

The model estimated tax from broadly described wage and investment income instead of computing the traced Wisconsin tax base: $35,012.56 AGI minus the exact $12,067.66 standard deduction and $700 exemption. Applying the 2026 brackets to the resulting $22,244.90 yields $843.66, not $943.

The model correctly excluded Social Security but incorrectly carried the $14,980 private pension into Alabama taxable income after deductions and exemptions. The model included the adult dependent’s earnings in the married couple’s joint taxable income, inflating taxable income from $80,208.25 to $124,708. It also denied the $500 nonrefundable other-dependent credit that reduces tentative tax.

The model included the 27-year-old dependent’s separately taxed earnings in the couple’s Missouri taxable income, producing its stated $124,708 base. The joint return instead starts from $115,097.88 of IRS gross income for the two primary filers and reaches $79,776.80 of Missouri taxable income after adjustments and deductions.

The model applied a 20% child and dependent care credit rate to $6,000 of expenses, producing $1,200 instead of the applicable 32% rate and $1,920 credit. The model treated the income threshold as dispositive and omitted California's universal free school meals pathway. Because the household has one K–12 student, that pathway provides positive free-meal support regardless of the household's 5.03 federal-poverty-guideline ratio.

The model used an approximate 1.2% California SDI rate instead of the applicable 1.3% rate. It also failed to reconcile its stated component calculation: 7.65% federal FICA plus 1.2% SDI on $165,000 equals $14,602.50, not $14,312.40.

The model used California’s $11,412 standard deduction instead of the larger $20,476.57 itemized deduction generated by the charitable contributions and real-estate taxes. It therefore overstated taxable income and the resulting tax after exemption credits.

The model treated low earnings as zero SNAP net income and awarded the full $785 maximum every month. The allowable deductions leave $237.09 of monthly net income, requiring a $71.10 monthly household contribution, and the applicable maximum also rises to $802.60 in later months.

The model incorrectly converted the head's generic disability status into SSI categorical eligibility. Because no member has the SSI-specific aged, blind, or disabled status, the head cannot receive SSI, and applying earned-income exclusions or a federal maximum benefit is inapplicable.
The model applied a 10% Montana EITC rate instead of the 2026 rate of 20%. Applying 20% to the $2,365.89 federal EITC yields $473.18.

The model reduced the family-of-three payment standard by an overstated amount of countable earnings, yielding $359.50 per month. Montana’s applicable earned-income disregards leave a substantially higher TANF payment, totaling $6,064.96 annually.

The model incorrectly deducted the spouse's $7,989 employer-sponsored insurance premium from gross wages even though the prompt states $46,000 of gross wages and does not identify that premium as a pretax wage reduction. The model incorrectly subtracted the spouse's $7,989 employer-sponsored insurance premium from FICA wages, reducing the payroll-tax base from $87,200 to $79,211. The model used $79,212 as state income, exactly $87,201 minus the spouse's $7,989 employer-sponsored insurance premium. It incorrectly treated that coverage premium as an additional deduction from reported gross wages, understating taxable income by $7,989.

The model omitted New York's refundable real property tax credit and incorrectly required some other stated basis for a refundable credit. The taxpayer's age and rent establish the relevant eligibility pathway, and the renter formula yields $375.

The model left approximately $1,712.50 of taxable income despite AGI of only $15,439.75 and a 2026 single standard deduction that already exceeds AGI. Taxable income must be floored at zero before applying the 10% bracket, so its $171.25 regular-tax calculation misapplied the deductions.

The model treated being elderly and disabled as establishing a Wisconsin aged/disabled Medicaid pathway and proceeded directly to income budgeting. The model incorrectly included unspecified “taxable survivor-benefit income” in the Wisconsin tax base and therefore computed residual tax after deductions and exemptions. Applying Wisconsin’s income subtractions, standard deduction, and exemptions to the listed income yields zero tax before refundable credits.

The model incorrectly treated qualifying children as necessary for Wisconsin refundable-credit eligibility. The 85-year-old disabled renter qualifies for the homestead credit, which yields $271.39 from $1,440 of imputed property tax and $20,590 of household income.

The model calculated refundable CTC as 15% of the full $30,000 self-employment income above $2,500, producing $4,125. It failed to subtract the deductible half of self-employment tax from earned income, which reduces refundable CTC to $3,807.09.
The model identified the correct 92.35% net-earnings factor and 15.3% combined rate but multiplied them incorrectly. Applying its own stated formula gives $4,238.865, not $4,241.

The model hard-coded a $659 monthly benefit and multiplied it by 12. Applying each month's 2026 maximum allotment and deduction parameters produces month-specific benefits that sum to $8,020.55, so the fixed $659 estimate understates the applicable schedule.

The model included the $6,323 estate-income input in AGI and omitted both the $198.35 traditional IRA deduction and the $1,264.59 qualified business income deduction. Although it applied the $387 nonitemizer charitable deduction, those AGI and deduction errors overstated taxable income and tax.

The model asserted that it applied the 2026 Ohio rate schedule but produced $4,418, which does not result from applying that schedule to the traced taxable income of $161,521.64. The model collapsed the disability pathway into a receipt-of-SSDI test. Disability income alone is insufficient: this 57-year-old has no listed fact establishing satisfaction of Medicare's disability waiting-period requirement or another under-65 qualifying condition.

The model stopped after concluding that countable income was too high to yield a positive benefit under the ordinary 30%-of-net-income formula. It failed to apply the minimum allotment required for this otherwise eligible one-person household, which yields $287.68 for the year.

The model incorrectly characterized the head’s MAGI as within Louisiana’s expansion-adult Medicaid limit. PolicyEngine computes MAGI at 2.13 times the Federal Poverty Level, above that pathway’s limit, and the head qualifies through no alternative Medicaid category.

The model applied 5.3% to the entire $139,270 amount and failed to exclude the $9,840 head-of-household zero-rate bracket. It also stopped before the additional $800 qualified business income deduction, leaving taxable income $1,000 above the traced $138,270 figure.
The model applied an outdated or incorrect $120 per-person grocery-credit amount instead of Idaho's 2026 amount of $155. Applying $155 to all three qualifying household members yields $465.

The model both used the wrong $12,500 standard deduction instead of $12,835 and subtracted an inapplicable $4,500 personal exemption. Louisiana taxable income is $10,171.18 without that personal-exemption subtraction, yielding $305.14 at 3%.

The model restricted the SALT deduction to the listed $26,460 of real-estate taxes and omitted the additional deductible state and local tax calculated under the 2026 rules. The correct SALT amount is $36,233.77, so total itemized deductions are $64,443.77 and taxable income is $134,061.22.

The model did not compute the stated itemized deductions correctly: mortgage interest of $28,210 plus real estate taxes of $26,460 gives a $54,670 deduction and taxable income of $143,835. Applying Maryland's rate schedule to that base yields $6,936.34, not $7,025.

Although the model counted Social Security and SSI, its $165 monthly result corresponds to net income around $1,270 and therefore fails to apply the full $354 of SNAP deductions that yields $1,157. The resulting expected contribution was too high, and the model also used one flat monthly allotment instead of the applicable parameter changes during 2026.

The model used a $1,486 monthly SSI couple payment standard instead of $1,491. Subtracting the correctly identified $610 of monthly countable SSDI from that incorrect standard yielded $876 rather than $881, understating annual SSI by $60.

The model wrongly treated earnings, children, or another special circumstance as necessary for every Arizona refundable credit. It omitted the increased excise tax credit available to this married-filing-jointly two-adult Arizona unit, which yields $50.

The model applied the ordinary SNAP resource limit directly to the $19,828 bank balance and stopped the eligibility calculation. Pennsylvania’s TANF non-cash categorical-eligibility pathway governs here, and the household’s $19,827.65 of counted assets, including $3,570 of vehicle value, remains below the applicable categorical limits.

The model stopped after applying Pennsylvania’s 3.07% rate to $940 and omitted Pennsylvania Tax Forgiveness. At this household’s low eligibility income, the Tax Forgiveness credit offsets the calculated $28.86 liability in full, yielding $0 state income tax before refundable credits.

The model undercomputed deductible Massachusetts income tax within the $14,127.41 state-and-local-tax itemized deduction and omitted the $1.24 qualified business income deduction. Its result leaves taxable income about $141.42 too high, which produces exactly the $33.94 excess tax at the 24% marginal rate.

The model correctly computed the $13,387.65 federal Social Security and Medicare total but applied an excessive Massachusetts PFML withholding of $962.51 instead of $805.01. Adding the correct mandatory employee PFML contribution yields $14,192.66.

The model did not derive the traced $169,058 Part B taxable income before exemption and instead applied 5% to an ad hoc base after a maximum renter deduction. Massachusetts requires $164,658 of Part B taxable income after the $4,400 exemption plus separate Part A taxation of $110.12 in dividends.

The model miscomputed the interaction between ordinary taxable income and the preferential-rate qualified-dividend and long-term-capital-gain layer. The model omitted the $411.91 New York paid family leave contribution and $31.20 disability benefits contribution, and it also understated federal FICA by rounding the component calculation to $7,654 instead of $7,664.92. Using the traced federal components and adding both mandatory state payroll taxes yields $8,108.03.

The model treated $5,977.08 as the head-of-household tax generated directly from taxable income, misapplying New York’s graduated rate schedule. On $105,385.15, the main tax is $5,442.17, and the distinct supplemental-tax calculation adds only $156.38.

The model incorrectly excluded the one-year-old child from the Empire State Child Credit age range and treated income as eliminating all refundable New York credits. The child qualifies for a $1,000 base credit reduced to $290.50 by phase-out, and the household also receives a $360 refundable Child and Dependent Care Credit.

The model correctly excluded the retirement income and Social Security and computed $51.42 of gross Pennsylvania tax on interest, but omitted Pennsylvania tax forgiveness. That nonrefundable relief eliminates the gross tax, so state income tax before refundable credits is $0.

The model did not apply the exact 2026 head-of-household parameters to the traced taxable income. The model omitted the $3,467.43 overtime income deduction and failed to apply the full $9,568.44 qualified business income deduction calculated as 20% of $47,842.18. Its $7,530 answer corresponds to taxable income materially above the correct $60,964.27.

The model explicitly declared the 22-year-old eligible for the childless EITC, overlooking the age-25 minimum for tax year 2026. The phase-in calculation is irrelevant because failure of the age test makes the EITC $0.

The model applied only the standard and overtime deductions, omitting the $926.16 pre-tax traditional 401(k) contribution and $43.28 traditional IRA above-the-line deduction from the AGI computation. The model correctly calculated both FICA components but added or transcribed them incorrectly: $1,674.00 plus $391.50 equals $2,065.50, not $2,457.00. Its numeric output contradicts its own stated derivation.

The model used an unspecified estimate of deductions rather than the exact Wisconsin standard deduction of $12,067.66 and $700 personal exemption. Those amounts reduce $35,012.56 of AGI to $22,244.90, and the 2026 Wisconsin brackets produce $843.66 rather than $860.

The model overstated joint taxable income by $4,999.75, using $85,208 instead of $80,208.25. It failed to apply the full 2026 married-filing-jointly standard deduction of $32,200 together with the engine’s $2,689.62 of allowable student-loan-interest and traditional-IRA deductions.

The model applied exemptions and a low-income tax reduction to the remaining self-employment income instead of first applying New Jersey's minimum gross-income threshold. The model netted the $15,540 taxable private pension against the $11,100 farm rental loss and then applied only the personal and senior exemptions. The model both used an obsolete 10% Montana EITC rate and incorrectly treated the five-year-old as generating a $1,200 Montana young-child credit. Only the Montana EITC applies, at 20% of $2,365.89, yielding $473.18.

The model invented countable SSI income even though no SSI receipt is listed and the household’s $2,800 in bank assets exceeds the individual SSI resource limit. The model improperly claimed a phased-down qualified auto-loan-interest deduction despite the absence of facts establishing that the vehicle and loan satisfy that deduction's requirements. Its $13,198.60 answer is exactly $220 below the correct tax, consistent with subtracting an unsupported $1,000 deduction from income taxed at the 22% marginal rate.

The model incorrectly treated the $7,989 employer-sponsored insurance premium as an above-the-line reduction of the stated gross wages. The model explicitly excluded the $7,989 employer-sponsored insurance premium from joint income even though the separately reported gross wages are not reduced by that health-coverage input. The half self-employment-tax deduction on $1 does not explain the shortfall; the erroneous premium exclusion reduced taxable income from $61,701 to about $53,712.

The model overlooked that being an elderly one-person renter is itself the relevant pathway to New York's refundable real property tax credit. Earnings and children are not required for this credit, whose formula produces $375 from the listed age and rent.

The model denied SNAP solely because countable net income exceeded the ordinary elderly or disabled household limit. It omitted categorical eligibility through TANF non-cash assistance, under which the household receives the minimum allotment when the standard benefit formula falls below that floor.

The model overlooked the Wisconsin homestead-credit pathway for an elderly or disabled renter and incorrectly inferred that a one-adult household had no qualifying refundable credit. The head's age, disability, Wisconsin residence, rent, and income establish eligibility and produce $271.39.

The model's $4,125 Additional Child Tax Credit equals 15% of unreduced $30,000 self-employment income above $2,500. It omitted the deduction for half of self-employment tax from earned income, so it overstated refundable CTC by $317.91.

The model hard-coded a $659 monthly benefit and multiplied it by 12. The correct month-by-month 2026 SNAP schedule yields benefits totaling $8,020.55, so its fixed monthly estimate understates the annual result.

It also used only the $16,100 standard deduction, failing to subtract the $387 nonitemizer charitable deduction and $1,264.59 qualified business income deduction before applying the ordinary-income and preferential rates.

The model incorrectly subtracted the $4,245 traditional 401(k) contribution from the FICA wage base. Applying 6.2% Social Security and 1.45% employee Medicare rates to the full $100,000 of wages yields $7,650, while its $7,325.26 is exactly 7.65% of the incorrectly reduced $95,755 base.

The model treated the ordinary gross-income limit as disqualifying and also counted farm-rent income that the SNAP computation excludes. The model mechanically applied the 92.35% net-earnings factor and 15.3% self-employment-tax rate to partnership or S-corporation income. Those steps apply only after identifying net earnings from self-employment; here that base is zero.

The $4,658 answer is consistent with applying Virginia's rates to nearly all Virginia AGI while failing to subtract the $19,419.94 itemized deduction and the full $1,730 of personal and aged exemptions. Those deductions reduce taxable income to $65,593.83 before the Virginia brackets are applied.

The model used an approximate high-income Arkansas calculation rather than the exact $8,189.05 joint-filer bracket liability and full $205 nonrefundable-credit subtraction. Its reference only to dependent credits failed to account explicitly for the two-adult personal credits and $60 of additional qualified-individual credits required to reach $7,984.05.

The model restricted shelter costs to the listed mortgage interest and property tax and omitted the applicable SNAP shelter-cost standard, including the utility allowance. That understated the uncapped excess-shelter deduction for an elderly or disabled household and inflated the expected contribution, producing $123 instead of about $240 per month.

The model applied the $20 SSI general income exclusion only once to annual Social Security income, producing $2,780 of countable income. SSI applies that exclusion monthly, totaling $240 annually and yielding $2,560 of countable income and $9,368 of annual SSI.

The model calculated only the $90 New York household credit based on filing status, income, and absence of children, while omitting the refundable real property tax credit. The age-74 homeowner's $1,633.50 property-tax burden yields the requested $375 state refundable-credit total.

The model applied an income limit above Virginia’s applicable 2026 Medicaid threshold for a 14-year-old and therefore treated 192% FPL as qualifying. It conflated the broader children’s coverage range, including CHIP, with the narrower Medicaid income pathway.

The model applied a child Medicaid income limit above 192% FPL to the 11-year-old. It therefore treated the household MAGI as within Virginia's Medicaid threshold when it exceeds the applicable limit.

The model approximated the EITC at $785 rather than applying the phaseout precisely to obtain $764.23. Its larger error was fixing the refundable child credit at $1,656 instead of calculating the $2,089.77 refundable CTC left after the nonrefundable credit is used.

It invented two $500 Virginia child credits that do not apply in this calculation and failed to apply the actual $152.85 nonrefundable Virginia EITC. Its $1,101.67 also does not equal the $2,011.94 bracket tax less the credits it claimed.

The model incorrectly assigned a $785 federal EITC and then calculated Virginia’s refundable EITC as 20% of that amount. At approximately $55,000 of earned income for a head-of-household filer with two qualifying children in 2026, the federal EITC is fully phased out, so the Virginia refundable EITC base and credit are both zero.

The model incorrectly included the $1,750 state-tax refund in AGI and invoked a restored personal exemption, although no personal-exemption deduction applies in this 2026 calculation. Its stated taxable income of approximately $203,194 also cannot produce $48,134 under the applicable 2026 single-filer brackets, so it misapplied the rate schedule in addition to constructing taxable income incorrectly.

The model incorrectly subtracted the $617 traditional 401(k) deferral from both Social Security and Medicare wages even though traditional 401(k) contributions remain subject to FICA. It also omitted Connecticut's $827.98 mandatory paid-leave contribution, while the correct federal components are $10,267.01 and $2,401.16 on the full $165,597 of wages.

The model explicitly denied Social Security relief and calculated from roughly $234,414, but Connecticut allows a $25,140 Social Security subtraction for this household. After that subtraction, the state AGI is $207,495.12, and the schedule plus recapture adjustments yields $11,917.18.

The model treated disability and deductible medical expenses as sufficient to reduce SNAP net income to zero without first enforcing the gross-income limit. The household fails the one-person gross-income test, so the medical deduction and maximum-benefit calculation never apply.

The model did not apply the full deduction and qualified-dividend computation: taxable income is $43,446.18, and the $1,824 of qualified dividends falls in the 0% capital-gains band rather than being taxed through the ordinary brackets. Its $4,957 estimate is consistent with taxing nearly all taxable income at ordinary rates instead of separating the qualified dividends.

The model described the Social Security subtraction generically but did not apply the full $32,177.18 of Minnesota subtractions—especially the $4,977.18 charitable subtraction—or the complete deduction sequence that reduces taxable income to $19,219. Its $2,640 answer therefore taxes an overstated pension, IRA, and investment-income base.

The model's tax amount incorporates the child's $45,000 of wages into the parents' joint taxable income even though those wages belong to the child's separate tax unit. It also used a $30,000 standard deduction instead of the applicable $32,200 standard deduction plus the $2,000 non-itemizer charitable deduction, so it overstated both the parents' income base and taxable income.

The model calculated only federal employee FICA and omitted Pennsylvania's mandatory employee unemployment compensation contribution. Adding the $101.50 state payroll tax to its $11,092.50 FICA calculation yields $11,194.

The model treated everyone living in the household as one Pennsylvania income-tax unit and included the child’s $45,000 of wages. Excluding the child’s separately taxed earnings leaves the parents’ $100,002 base, to which the 3.07% rate produces $3,070.06.
